tldr: Part III of a six-paper condensed-mathematics program on topological phases of matter. It defines the uniformly gapped substack Gap_{d,G} of the condensed moduli stack Ham_{d,G} of G-symmetric quasi-local Hamiltonians -- families whose thermodynamic gap is bounded away from zero simultaneously over the whole profinite probe -- and treats the Cubitt-Perez-Garcia-Wolf (CPW) undecidability of the spectral gap as the paper's governing constraint: existence of a gap is a defining hypothesis, not a decidable property, so the paper proves stability rather than attempting detection. Three theorems from cited technology: the finite-volume n-gap is 2-Lipschitz in the interaction norm, giving unconditional openness of the gapped locus at every finite resolution (Theorem III-A, via Weyl's inequality plus finite-volume norm domination); on the frustration-free/LTQO stratum, the Bravyi-Hastings-Michalakis, Michalakis-Zwolak, and Nachtergaele-Sims-Young stability theorems supply a size-independent perturbation threshold, recast as condensed-openness of Gap_{d,G} along quasi-local directions, with an explicit spectral-flow realization of the W-equivalence class (Theorem III-B); and a uniform gap forces a correlation length bounded uniformly over the profinite base, which provably fails for merely pointwise-gapped families (Theorem III-C). Three explicitly scoped conjectures (III-1 openness on the physical stratum with finite-quotient detection, III-2 uniform-gap descent along profinite disorder hulls, III-3 identification of quasi-adiabatic path components with Ogata's operator-algebraic phase classification) mark what remains open. A transverse-field Ising chain computation (exact diagonalization plus Haskell/QuickCheck) renders the gapless discriminant and the Lipschitz stability numerically, while explicitly stating that no finite computation decides the thermodynamic gap.
plain_language_summary:
A quantum system made of many interacting particles (spins on a lattice, or the electrons in an insulator, say) is called 'gapped' if there is a definite minimum amount of energy separating its ground state from any excited state, and this gap survives as the system is made arbitrarily large. Physicists organize gapped systems into 'phases': two gapped systems belong to the same phase if one can be continuously deformed into the other while the gap never closes. This paper is the third installment of a six-part mathematical research program that reworks this classification using 'condensed mathematics' (Clausen and Scholze's framework for handling continuously varying families, profinite limits, and disordered systems). The object under study here is the collection of all 'uniformly gapped' systems -- the substack Gap_{d,G} sitting inside the moduli stack of all Hamiltonians -- and the paper's job is to show that this collection is a well-behaved mathematical object: that small changes to a gapped system keep it gapped, and that a gap has good structural consequences.

Standing over the whole enterprise is a hard limitative theorem, due to Cubitt, Perez-Garcia and Wolf, that the paper treats as its central organizing constraint. They constructed an explicit, computer-describable family of two-dimensional lattice models for which the question 'does this system have an energy gap in the limit of an infinitely large lattice?' is undecidable: there is provably no algorithm, however clever, that can answer yes-or-no correctly for every member of the family. This is not a statement about currently missing techniques; it is a mathematical impossibility result, as hard as the halting problem, embedded directly into the low-energy physics. The paper's response is to stop treating 'does this system have a gap' as something to be computed or verified in general, and instead to treat gappedness as a standing assumption: one is handed a family of systems declared gapped, and the paper asks what can be said about its neighborhood, its correlations, and its behavior across the family, given that assumption. This is why the paper is about stability rather than existence.

Within that constraint, the paper proves three theorems using existing, cited technology. First, for any single finite-size piece of the lattice, the energy gap changes only mildly (in a precise, quantified sense called Lipschitz continuity) when the interaction is perturbed slightly; consequently, at finite size, being gapped is an open condition -- a small enough nudge cannot destroy it -- and this fact is completely unconditional, because a finite matrix's eigenvalues are always computable and undecidability bites only in the infinite-size limit. Second, for a well-studied subclass of models -- 'frustration-free' systems with a property called local topological quantum order (LTQO), which includes textbook examples like Kitaev's toric code and AKLT chains -- decades-old stability theorems show that the gap survives small perturbations by an amount that does not shrink as the system grows. The paper repackages this as saying that around every such model there is a genuine open neighborhood, in the space of all interactions, that stays inside the uniformly-gapped collection; it also shows explicitly how the perturbed ground states are related to the original ones by a smooth, local transformation (a 'spectral flow'), which is the precise sense in which they count as 'the same phase.' This theorem does not apply to every gapped system -- notably, chiral systems like Chern insulators are gapped and stable but are not of the frustration-free type covered here, and the paper is explicit that it says nothing about them. Third, the paper shows that a gap which is uniform across an entire family of systems (bounded away from zero simultaneously across all members, not just present at each one individually) forces correlations between distant points to decay exponentially at a rate that is also uniform across the family, and it proves that this uniformity genuinely requires the 'simultaneously bounded away from zero' hypothesis: a family where each member is gapped but the gap shrinks to zero somewhere in the family enjoys no such uniform bound.

Beyond what it can prove, the paper states three explicit, numbered open conjectures, each scoped so that proving it would not contradict the undecidability theorem: that the openness property of the second theorem holds more broadly, on a suitably restricted 'physical' class of systems detectable by looking at finite approximations; that uniform gappedness 'descends' correctly through the profinite limits used to model disordered or aperiodic systems; and that the 'same phase' equivalence built from smooth deformations exactly matches an existing, independently developed classification of gapped phases in operator algebra, due to Ogata.

The paper closes with a worked numerical example: the transverse-field Ising chain, a simple one-dimensional spin chain solvable exactly by an established technique (the Jordan-Wigner transformation). Exact diagonalization of small chains locates the point where the gap closes, shows the gap growing linearly away from that point, and numerically confirms the Lipschitz stability bound from the first theorem, with the computation implemented in Haskell and checked by automated property-based tests. But the paper is explicit that this example illustrates structure rather than demonstrating that finite computation can decide gappedness in general: the Ising chain's gap is known exactly only because the model happens to be exactly solvable by an independent analytic method, and for a generic family of lattice models the undecidability theorem guarantees that no amount of finite computation could settle the question.
key_contributions: Formalizes the uniformly gapped substack Gap_{d,G} of the condensed Hamiltonian moduli stack (Def. 'gap'), with the inf_s-over-the-probe condition doing the essential work, and proves its basic sheaf-theoretic packaging as a filtered union of subpresheaves stable under restriction (Prop. 'union').

Proves Theorem III-A: the finite-volume n-gap is 2C_Lambda-Lipschitz in the interaction F-norm (via Weyl's eigenvalue inequality plus finite-volume norm domination), giving unconditional continuity of the gap and openness of the finite-resolution gapped locus -- the one place where openness holds without any stratum restriction, since undecidability is purely a thermodynamic-limit phenomenon.

Proves Theorem III-B: recasts the Bravyi-Hastings-Michalakis, Michalakis-Zwolak, and Nachtergaele-Sims-Young stability theorems as condensed-openness of Gap_{d,G} along quasi-local perturbation directions on the frustration-free/LTQO stratum, with an explicit companion result (Prop. 'flow') constructing the quasi-adiabatic spectral flow that realizes the W-equivalence between the perturbed and unperturbed ground-state spaces.

Proves Theorem III-C: a uniform gap over a profinite family forces a correlation length bounded uniformly over the whole base (via Hastings-Koma/Nachtergaele-Sims clustering plus the uniform Lieb-Robinson velocity of Part I), and proves the necessity of the uniformity hypothesis directly (Cor. 'pointwise-fail'): merely pointwise-gapped families with a degenerating infimum gap admit no such uniform bound.

States three explicitly numbered, individually-scoped conjectures (III-1 openness on the physical stratum with finite-quotient detection; III-2 uniform-gap descent along profinite disorder hulls Omega=F^{Z^d}; III-3 identification of quasi-adiabatic-continuation path components with Ogata's operator-algebraic gapped-phase classification), each anchored to a proved result of the paper and each explicitly checked against the CPW undecidability wall.

Supplies a worked numerical illustration (transverse-field Ising chain, exact diagonalization up to N=8 plus a Haskell/QuickCheck implementation) that renders the gapless discriminant Sigma={g=1} and the Theorem III-A Lipschitz bound concretely, paired with an explicit statement (Sec. 'numerics-cannot') of what the finite computation cannot establish about the thermodynamic gap.
audience: Mathematical physicists and operator algebraists working on rigorous stability theory for gapped quantum lattice systems (Bravyi-Hastings-Michalakis/Michalakis-Zwolak/Nachtergaele-Sims-Young-type results, Lieb-Robinson bounds, quasi-adiabatic continuation); researchers applying Clausen-Scholze condensed mathematics outside arithmetic geometry; readers interested in how the Cubitt-Perez-Garcia-Wolf undecidability result constrains what a classification program can claim; and readers of the companion six-part series who need this paper's stability and clustering results (Theorems III-A - III-C) as the input Part IV's stabilization and Part V's realizability constructions build on. The introduction, undecidability discussion, and transverse-field Ising worked example are largely self-contained for anyone with graduate-level background in quantum many-body theory or operator algebras; full appreciation of the condensed-openness framing benefits from familiarity with Parts I-II.
